faq.q: When does it make sense to buy vs rent in India?
faq.a: Buying makes sense when: price-to-rent ratio < 20 (property price < 20× annual rent), you plan to stay 7+ years, EMI is less than 40% of income, and property appreciation > home loan rate. Renting makes sense in expensive cities (Mumbai, Bangalore) where rental yield is 2–3% but loan rates are 8.5%.

faq.q: What is the price-to-rent ratio in Indian cities?
faq.a: Mumbai: 50–80× (property very expensive relative to rent). Bangalore: 35–50×. Delhi: 40–60×. Pune: 30–45×. Tier 2 cities: 15–25×. A ratio below 20 generally favours buying; above 30 favours renting and investing the down payment.

faq.q: Is buying property always a good investment in India?
faq.a: Not always. Over long periods, Indian real estate has appreciated at 6–8% p.a. in major cities. But equity mutual funds have returned 12–15% p.a. over 20 years. The emotional and security value of owning a home is real but hard to quantify. Factor in maintenance (1–2% p.a.), property tax, and illiquidity.

examples.heading: ₹80 lakh flat in Bangalore — buy or rent?
examples.text: Monthly rent for equivalent flat: ₹25,000. EMI at 8.5% for 20 years on ₹64 lakh (80%): ₹55,669. Monthly cost difference: ₹30,669. Investing ₹30,669/month at 12% for 20 years = ₹2.64 crore. Property appreciation (6%): ₹80 lakh → ₹2.57 crore. Very close — emotional preference decides.

examples.heading: Tier 2 city — buy wins clearly
examples.text: ₹40 lakh property in Pune, monthly rent ₹12,000, EMI ₹26,000. Monthly extra for buying: ₹14,000. At 6% appreciation, property → ₹1.28 crore in 20 years. Renting and investing ₹14,000/month at 12% = ₹1.2 crore. Buying slightly better — plus down payment grows separately.
